data.title: The US owes the people of Hong Kong an explanation
data.description:
Six years ago, the U.S. drew a line on Hong Kong.  

After Beijing imposed a sweeping national security law on the city, criminalizing dissent and effectively ending the “one country, two systems” framework that had defined Hong Kong since the 1997 handover, President Trump signed Executive Order 13936. It declared a national emergency, stripped Hong Kong of the preferential trade and export-control treatment it had long enjoyed as a distinct customs territory, and opened the door to sanctions on officials responsible for dismantling the city’s freedoms. 

That line just quietly disappeared.

On July 14, the national emergency underlying Trump’s order lapsed. There was no announcement from the White House, no policy speech explaining the reasoning, just silence. The administration simply declined to file the routine renewal notice it had filed every year since 2020.  

It was Beijing, not Washington, that told the world first. China’s Ministry of Commerce announced the news on July 17, framing it as the fulfillment of commitments the U.S. reportedly made during trade talks in Madrid last September. The Treasury Department later confirmed the decision, but has offered no public explanation of what was agreed to or why. 

The American public, and the people of Hong Kong, deserve that explanation.  

The practical stakes are real. Sanctions relief now extends to nine officials directly responsible for the crackdown on Hong Kong, including Justice Secretary Paul Lam, National Security Department Chief Sonny Au, and former police commissioners Raymond Siu and Stephen Lo. These individuals have spent years enforcing a legal apparatus that has jailed pro-democracy activists, shuttered independent newsrooms and, as recently as this spring, allowed authorities to freeze the assets of imprisoned publisher and British citizen Jimmy Lai. The lapse may also pave the way for Hong Kong to regain the preferential trade status and export-control exemptions the order revoked in 2020.

None of this reflects an improvement on the ground. Just this year, Hong Kong officials implemented new national-security offenses criminalizing refusal to hand over passwords to police, sweeping new powers letting the city’s leader certify any case as a national-security matter beyond judicial review, and continued asset seizures reaching dissidents abroad.  

Judging by the very standard the order itself set in 2020 — an “unusual and extraordinary threat“ to Hong Kong’s autonomy — nothing has changed except Washington’s willingness to say so. 

There is a human cost that rarely makes it into a Treasury statement. Thousands of Hong Kongers have come to the United States in recent years, many fleeing the very crackdown the national emergency was designed to answer. They came here believing that America, its laws and its willingness to stand up to Beijing was a genuine refuge from a government that had already shown it would reach across borders to silence its critics. Hong Kong authorities have placed bounties on pro-democracy activists living overseas, and have launched transnational repression campaigns reaching diaspora communities from Melbourne to London to right here in the U.S.  

For many of these families, Washington’s silence sends a signal that the country they trusted to protect them may no longer be willing to do so.

This was not a reassessment prompted by improvements in Hong Kong. To all indications, it was a trade concession, negotiated behind closed doors, confirmed not by the administration that made it but by the government that benefited from it. The non-renewal of the national emergency does not just fail to hold Beijing accountable — it hands the Chinese Communist Party a win it has not earned.  

Congress and the public deserve real answers. What, specifically, did the United States receive from Beijing in exchange? Were human rights conditions ever part of that negotiation, or was Hong Kong simply a chip on a larger table involving tariffs and technology? 

Congress does not have to accept silence as an answer. It has the tools to find out: hearings, document requests, and the oversight authority it already holds over U.S.-China policy. Lawmakers from both parties supported the Hong Kong Human Rights and Democracy Act and the Hong Kong Autonomy Act by overwhelming bipartisan margins because they understood that human rights and trade policy should not be interchangeable. That principle is worth defending now, not just in 2020. 

The administration must reconsider this decision and ensure that sanctions on the Hong Kong officials responsible for dismantling the city’s judicial independence and civil liberties remain firmly in place.  

The people of Hong Kong, the judges forced from the bench, the journalists who have fled or been jailed, activists like Jimmy Lai still sitting in prison, and the families who crossed an ocean believing in the promise of American freedom, did not choose this moment of American retreat. They deserve better than to be a bargaining chip in a trade deal negotiated on their behalf but without their voice.

At minimum, they deserve an honest explanation. 

Megan Khoo is policy director at Hong Kong Watch.

data.title: Explosions heard near US base in Iraq after Pentagon finishes latest strikes against Iran
data.description:
Explosive-laden drones targeted a military base in northern Iraq that hosts American forces on Friday, hours after the U.S. military launched its 13th consecutive night of strikes against Iran.

Kurdish security officials said coalition forces shot down five drones over Erbil, Iraq — where troops were stationed at the Erbil International Airport.

A journalist for The Associated Press reported hearing at least seven explosions and seeing four plumes of black smoke near the city’s airport. Officials confirmed that “the incident caused no casualties or damage.”

The strikes came hours after the U.S. military, which has at least 2,500 troops stationed in Iraq, confirmed it completed another night of strikes against Iranian military sites it says were used to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

“This is the 13th consecutive night of strikes aimed to hold Iran accountable and diminish threats from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps to commercial shipping,” a Pentagon official announced Thursday.

Warning sirens were also heard in Bahrain, which hosts the U.S. Navy’s Fifth Fleet. Bahrain’s Interior Ministry confirmed the sirens in a series of warnings posted to social platform X.

Bahrain’s military confirmed that it intercepted several attacks by Iran in a post on social media.

“The air defenses of the Bahrain Defense Force intercepted and destroyed a number of Iranian aerial aggressions,” the military said in a statement.

Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels also claimed responsibility for attacks against two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, prompting the U.S. military to take action amid escalating tensions over commercial and oil shipping routes.

President Trump on Thursday warned the Houthis would face “major military punishment” if attacks on commercial vessels continue. He also threatened to use frozen Iranian assets held by the U.S. to pay for damage to ships targeted during the conflict.

“Any and all damages done to Ships, Cargo, or anything related thereto, will be paid for by Iranian Money that the United States has in its possession, and controls,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Live updates: Trump's tariffs set to kick in; White House Correspondents' dinner re-do
data.description:
President Trump’s 10 percent tariffs are set to expire on Friday, but U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced new levies on Thursday to replace them.

The fresh import taxes –– ranging from 10 percent to 12.5 percent –– will hit 60 countries, accounting for 99 percent of U.S. imports. They are pegged to the elimination of forced labor. International trading partners ripped the new tariffs on Friday.

U.S. forces have launched strikes against Iran for nearly two weeks straight, and Republican defense hawks are urging Trump to unleash major combat operations. 

Trump will speak Friday evening at the rescheduled White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner. The WHCA said earlier this week it will honor a Secret Service agent who was pivotal in stopping a gunman who tried to assassinate Trump during the annual dinner this spring.

The House is now on a five-week recess, while the Senate is set to return Monday. And tension is rising between Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and the White House as the upper chamber struggles to pass Trump’s key priority: the SAVE America Act.

Follow live below.

data.title: Deadline to file claim in $2.5B settlement over Amazon Prime coming soon
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Those who were impacted by the alleged “deceptive methods” Amazon used for Prime subscriptions have just days to file a claim to receive part of a $2.5 billion settlement.

Amazon settled with the Federal Trade Commission last year after the company was accused of using “deceptive methods” to register millions of customers for paid Prime subscriptions, then making it difficult for those customers to cancel. Amazon agreed to pay a $2.5 billion settlement ($1.5 billion will fund customer refunds) and to “cease unlawful enrollment and cancellation practices for Prime.”

Amazon, which did not admit wrongdoing, previously said that while it has “always followed the law … this settlement allows us to move forward and focus on innovating for customers.”

Automatic refunds were sent out to some impacted customers already, and earlier this year, the claim process opened for other eligible Prime customers. That process closes in just a few days.

Who qualifies to file a claim?

You have to be a U.S. Prime member who signed up for Prime between June 23, 2019, and June 23, 2025. You also had to have “unintentionally” signed up with Amazon’s “challenged enrollment flow” or tried and failed to cancel your subscription. Further, you had to have used more than three Prime benefits, like Prime Music or Prime Video, but fewer than 10 between June 23, 2019, and June 23, 2025.

You don’t need to verify that you meet all three conditions. Instead, the FTC said Amazon would notify qualifying customers who have not already received automatic refunds. Those claim notices were delivered to mailboxes or emails and postmarked in late January.

You will need that notice to file a claim.

How do I file a claim?

You can do so online via the settlement website. You’ll need to enter the claim ID and PIN listed on your notice. Officials warned that the process does not require you to pay a fee or enter your Social Security number or Amazon login.

There is an option to file a claim without an ID or PIN.

You will be asked to enter your address, email, and phone number or email associated with the Amazon account. The form will then ask you multiple questions to confirm you are not “a bot or person attempting to file multiple claims on behalf of other purchasers.” Then you will be asked how you would like to receive your payment and to electronically sign the form before submitting.

How much will I receive, and when is the deadline?

You have until Monday, July 27, to submit the online claim form, according to the settlement administrators.

Payments, which are capped at $51 but could be less than $1, are expected to be disbursed later this year.

There is no opt-out option, but you can choose not to file a claim, officials said.

data.title: Todd Blanche is unfit to serve as attorney general
data.description:
At his confirmation hearing, acting Attorney General Todd Blanche told Congress a bald-faced lie. 

In the aftermath of President Trump’s effort to set up a $1.776 billion “anti-weaponization” fund, Sen. Alex Padilla (D-Calif.) pressed Blanche about whether he would commit to fighting any future claims for compensation from pardoned defendants involved in the January 6 Capitol riot. Blanche responded, “I can tell you we always vigorously defend our cases.”

I don’t know about January 6 defendants, but that’s certainly not true when it comes to President Trump himself.

The fund in question came to be out of a $10 billion lawsuit filed by Trump, claiming that the government was liable for releasing his tax information. The settlement, signed by Blanche himself, included a provision barring the government from prosecuting or pursuing any claims “known or unknown” against Trump, his companies, and his family members for anything that occurred prior to May 19, 2026. Tax returns are specifically mentioned.  

This extraordinary and unprecedented auto-settlement is simply a presidential self-pardon by the back door. 

As acting attorney general, Blanche didn’t defend this case vigorously. He didn’t defend this case at all. The government settled with Trump without filing an answer or raising any defenses, not even what appears to be a slam-dunk claim that the case should have been thrown out because the statute of limitations had expired. 

This is all as bad as it sounds. The judge in the case found that the Department of Justice had “abdicat[ed] its responsibility to zealously defend the interests of the United States” and that the settlement disregarded Justice Department policies “and accomplished objectives beyond those authorized, as well as those specifically prohibited, by law.” She even specified that her opinion should be sent to the New York state bar, where Blanche may face discipline.

There were a lot of questions about this settlement at Blanche’s recent confirmation. But the problem here isn’t just the details of the settlement. The problem is that Blanche was willing to settle.

The attorney general occupies a unique position in the constitutional firmament, representing the U.S. itself rather than the president. Consequently, one of the attorney general’s most important jobs is telling the president, when appropriate, “No.”

This happens more often than you might think. Attorney General John Ashcroft refused to sign off on a secret domestic spying program because he believed it was illegal. Not only did he resist intense pressure from then-President George W. Bush, he did so from his hospital bed shortly after undergoing emergency surgery.

Attorney General Jeff Sessions, much to Trump’s shock and annoyance during his first term in office, became a zealous defender of the rule of law and the independence of the Justice Department. Even Bill Barr stood up to the president when he finally found a line he could not cross.

Blanche clearly lacks this independence, and it makes him unfit to serve as attorney general. He couldn’t say “no” to Trump’s attempt to abuse the court system by filing a $10 billion sham lawsuit. He can’t even bring himself to say “no” to the president now that the court has declared that “the purported ‘settlement agreement'” is void. Despite the court’s order, Blanche continues to insist that Trump’s immunity deal is still in force. 

This isn’t about Trump’s policies or Blanche’s resume. It’s not about party politics. It’s about Blanche lacking an ethical backbone and the willingness to exercise independent judgment. Blanche gave the game away himself when asked by Sen. John Kennedy whether he was Trump’s friend. Blanche replied, “I’m his lawyer.” He quickly corrected himself by adding, “was his lawyer.” But slips don’t come much more Freudian than that. 

I understand why Trump, personally, is desperate to have Todd Blanche as his attorney general. It’s the same reason why Republican senators should refuse to confirm him. 

Even if you give garden-variety Republicans a pass, there are 21 Republican lawyers in the Senate, and every single one understands that Blanche is ethically and temperamentally unfit to be attorney general. The attorney general of the U.S. is supposed to be the country’s chief defender of the rule of law, not the president’s tool for subverting it. 

Should Blanche be confirmed, it will say far more about Senate Republicans than it does about Blanche. If congressional Republicans view their job as simply rubber-stamping Trump’s worst impulses, why are they there? That’s a question a lot of people will be asking come November.   

Chris Truax is an appellate attorney and charter member of the Society for the Rule of Law.

data.title: Senate Republican: American public 'growing weary' with Iran war
data.description:
Senate Republican Policy Committee Chair Shelley Moore Capito (R-W.Va.) said the American public is “growing weary” with the protracted military conflict with Iran, which President Trump initially said would last four to five weeks but instead has dragged on for nearly five months.  

“I do think that the American public’s growing weary,” Capito told NewsNation’s “The Hill with Blake Burman” in an interview.

She warned about thinning patience for the conflict when asked about fellow West Virginia Sen. Jim Justice’s (R-W.Va.) comments earlier in the week about the need to find an “off-ramp” to the war.

“There’s got to be an off-ramp, that’s all there is to it,” Justice said on Wednesday.

“Naturally all of us are concerned because we’ve lost three wonderful, wonderful service people in the last little while,” he continued, referring to three U.S. service members killed by Iranian strikes in the past week.

Capito told NewsNation that she’s hearing the same concerns about the war from constituents as Justice.

“Senator Justice and I represent the same folks. We can feel that in our local conversations. And I do think a promised shorter endgame has now been, timeline’s been exceeded. We’ve lost four of our service members,” the senator added. “That is always devastating to Americans and American families, not just those most closely affected.”

“And I think that’s where you begin to see the weariness creep in,” Capito said.

Eighteen U.S. service members have died so far in the conflict along with hundreds or even thousands of Iranian civilians.

Four U.S. soldiers recently killed by strikes in Jordan and Iraq came from states around the country, including Hawaii, Texas, New York and North Carolina.

Despite the growing public weariness with the conflict, GOP senators including Capito and Justice voted against advancing a war powers resolution Thursday that would have directed President Trump to withdraw U.S. troops from the conflict unless it is authorized by Congress.

The House on Thursday approved a non-binding resolution directing Trump to end the war after four Republicans joined Democrats in voting for it. Those Republicans were Reps. Tom Barrett (Mich.), Warren Davidson (Ohio), Thomas Massie (Ky.) and Brian Fitzpatrick (Pa.).

The House this week also passed on a party-line Republican vote a budget framework providing $73 billion in funding to help cover the costs of the war.

Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth testified to the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday that the conflict has cost taxpayers $37.5 billion so far.

Capito said that Senate Republicans will press the administration for more details about a possible endgame for the conflict as they consider an emergency supplemental defense spending package this summer.

“It’s part of our oversight to see how much this is being funded in for the Iranian situation. And where is the endgame,” she said.

The West Virginia Republican also noted that Hegseth’s answers about how to end the conflict were “vague” because of fast-moving events, such as the emergence of Iranian-backed Houthi militants in Yemen who are threatening oil transports through the Red Sea.

“And you know … he was vague on that because I think what we see is it’s popping up. There’s more. They’re attacking seven countries. The Houthis are involved in this,” she said.

data.title: US trading partners rip new Trump tariffs: 'Completely unjustified'
data.description:
U.S. trading partners including China, Japan and Australia pushed back on Friday against President Trump’s latest round of tariffs, arguing the levies are “completely unjustified.”

The new import taxes, ranging from 10 percent to 12.5 percent for 60 countries, took effect just after midnight on Friday and cover 99.4 percent of goods coming into the U.S.

The Trump administration announced the new measures Thursday, saying they were authorized under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 following a four-month investigation into claims of alleged forced labor in the supply chain.

Australian Trade Minister Don Farrell condemned the tariffs and denied allegations that the country had failed to combat modern slavery.

“We believe that amongst all of the countries in the world Australia does take the issue of slavery, modern slavery, seriously, and will continue to do that,” Farrell told reporters in Adelaide, Australia.

He added that the higher taxes are “completely unjustified” and that Australian officials “will continue to lobby the United States Trade Representative to remove all tariffs on Australian goods.”

New Zealand Prime Minister Christopher Luxon similarly described the tariffs as “extremely disappointing,” calling the action unjustified and harmful to trade.

“Extremely disappointing to see new US tariffs on New Zealand and 59 other countries,” he wrote late Thursday on social platform X. “The US investigation did not provide meaningful evidence to support claims in relation to forced labour. Tariffs are not the way — they drive up costs and uncertainty for businesses.”

China also expressed its longstanding condemnation of the additional levies, with spokesperson Lin Jian saying the country “opposes all forms of unilateral tariffs.”

“Tariff wars and trade wars do not serve any parties’ interests,” Lin said during a daily briefing.

Japan blasted the tariffs after its exports were hit with a 12.5 percent tariff. Chief Cabinet Secretary Minoru Kihara said Tokyo had an understanding that an earlier trade agreement with the U.S. meant no additional tariffs would be imposed beyond a previously negotiated 10 percent import duty.

“Our understanding is both sides are still committed to that,” Kihara said during a news conference.

“It is regrettable that the measure imposes tariffs on the grounds of non-existence of measures banning imports of goods made by forced labor, even though Japan’s industry and trade are in line with international rules,” he added.

According to The Associated Press, Singapore said it would continue to engage with the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative to seek alternatives, while South Korea said it would maintain close contact with Washington to preserve what it described as a mutual “balance of benefits.”

The fresh tariffs also come as the president’s 10 percent global tariff is set to expire Friday. He issued the baseline tax after the Supreme Court struck down the bulk of his emergency tariffs earlier this year.

data.title: A ship linked to China's military sailed from Baltimore. That should worry US workers.
data.description:
Last month, a ship docked at a refinery in the small town of Reserve, Louisiana, roughly a half-hour drive from New Orleans. The vessel departed from Baltimore, and sailed down the Atlantic coast, loaded with asphalt and other manufacturing material. 

On its face, nothing about this is remarkable — but the vessel’s arrival in New Orleans should outrage every American who cares about workers’ rights and national security. That’s because this is not an American ship. Jin Zhou Wan is owned and operated by a Chinese military cut-out. And under normal circumstances and United States law, its journey would be absolutely illegal.

The Jones Act prohibits foreign ships like Jin Zhou Wan from transporting goods between U.S. ports. For more than a century, the Jones Act has been one of the most effective pieces of industrial policy in American history, providing reliable domestic security, protecting maritime workers’ rights and supporting our overall maritime industry and infrastructure.

But since March, spurred by the start of the war with Iran, President Trump has waived Jones Act protections, welcoming foreign-flagged ships with foreign crews into America’s domestic commerce.

President Trump’s presumed intentions here are reasonable. He has been advised that opening domestic shipping lanes to foreign-flagged vessels and crews will bring down prices (particularly energy costs) by increasing supplies during a time of war. Unfortunately, the facts do not bear this out. 

The truth is that the waiver has failed to do the two things it was supposed to do. First, it has not opened new routes. According to a Navigistics Consulting report from the first months of the waiver, foreign vessels are largely sailing the same routes as legal Jones Act ships. Second, it has failed to reduce prices. Take gasoline: The report notes that only about 6.5 percent of U.S. gasoline moves on Jones Act vessels to begin with; the vast majority moves via pipeline. 

So, it is no surprise that a price analysis covering March 23 to June 1 found no credible evidence that the waiver delivered relief at the pump. In fact, on several routes analyzed — including New Orleans to Port Everglades — foreign-flag rates were actually higher than Jones Act rates.

What has the waiver done?

It has sharply increased traffic at American ports by vessels controlled by Chinese interests: Eighteen percent of vessels using the waiver can be tied to China. It has halted domestic maritime development that’s necessary to economic and military national security, for fear that any U.S. investment will be crushed by cheap foreign competition. And it has hurt American workers by bringing low-paid, untrained foreign labor here — threatening to do to our maritime industry what the North American Free Trade Agreement did to industrial manufacturing.

In the end, neither the consumer nor the crucial American maritime industry benefit from this waiver — but foreign-flag operators certainly do. This is not a partisan issue; in fact, support for the Jones Act is the rare position that still enjoys broad and durable bipartisan support. The Jones Act has strong support from the Democratic Party , and earlier this month, Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) and 51 of his Republican House members sent a letter to President Trump urging him not to extend the waiver.

If the president does not act soon, the arrival of Jin Zhou Wan will no longer be a remarkable occurrence. But the docking of ships owned and operated and built in America, by Americans, in American ports, may become a rare one. We must act now before it is too late.

Chris Westbook is vice president, Gulf Coast, at the Seafarers International Union.

data.title: Trump is fighting for the working class, unlike the DSA
data.description:
Who actually represents working-class Americans?  

It sure isn’t the Democratic Socialists of America, who are largely white, college-educated liberals. They claim to be fighting for that group they cannot even define. Asked who makes up the working class by a New York Times reporter, New York’s Mayor Zohran Mamdani offered a babbling brook of a response that appeared to include anyone who “works to afford their life.” His definition could arguably include $1,500 dollar-an-hour lawyers and millionaire investment bankers with fancy houses in the Hamptons. That is how deeply Mamdani understands the working, middle and professional classes.

Unions no longer seem to represent the working class. They are too busy struggling for survival and tagging along with leftists like activist Claire Valdez, who recently won the Democratic primary in New York’s 7th congressional district with the backing of the United Auto Workers. Valdez, a member of that union by virtue of her status as a grad student, hates and wants to abolish cars. She wants to redirect government money away from highways and toward public transportation. 

Do the assembly line workers at Ford agree with that?

The UAW mobilized thousands of members to deliver the district for Valdez, so she can “fight back against corporate greed and inequality,” according to UAW President Shawn Fain. The kind of inequality, presumably, that allowed Valdez to attend a fancy private art school in Chicago that costs $60,000 per year.  

The SEIU and AFL-CIO embrace transgender issues and support sanctuary cities — the very sort of woke policies that warm socialist hearts but probably don’t sit well with conservative-leaning working-class Americans. 

The AFL-CIO commits, for instance, to refusing “to allow billionaires and the politicians they fund to distract and divide the labor movement with attacks on transgender and nonbinary workers.” What rote nonsense.   

Democrats and their media allies won’t admit it, but the truth is that President Trump continues to be the strongest advocate for America’s working-class men and women. 

Democrats have historically held an advantage with blue-collar workers, but Trump changed all that when he ran for president in 2016. As Alan Abramowitz wrote for Larry Sabato’s Center for Politics, “It was the overwhelming support of white working class voters that enabled Democrats to dominate elections … during the 1930s and 1940s. Even after Dwight Eisenhower broke the Democratic lock on the White House in 1952, white working class votes allowed Democrats to dominate Congress and most state governments from the 1950s through the 1970s.”

That has changed. Today, Abramowitz writes, “the white working class comprises the electoral base of the Republican Party.” Exit polls show that Trump won roughly two-thirds of the vote among whites without a college degree in the last three elections, while he won less than half of white college graduates, formerly the mainstay of the Republican Party.   

Why the shift? Many reasons, including voters blaming Democrats for trade deals that sent jobs overseas, fury over lax immigration policies as well as cultural issues like those noted above. But beginning in 2016, Trump talked to working-class voters about things they care about, like protecting our borders, safe streets and demanding fair trade.   

In 2024, Trump campaigned for middle-class votes by promising, among other things, No Taxes on Tips and No Taxes on Overtime. Democrats accuse Trump of pandering only to the billionaire class, but those proposals were directed at waitresses and factory workers. Moreover, those ideas were not just campaign slogans; Trump and his GOP colleagues made sure they were included in last year’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act. 

Trump’s pro-business policies that were included in last year’s tax bill, like incentives for investment and his signature drive to cut red tape. These do not just benefit CEOs; they also lead to a growing economy, strong employment and higher wages. The conservative Committee to Unleash Prosperity reports that in the first 16 months of Trump’s current term, real incomes for the lowest 25 percent of earners increased by almost $2,100. They actually shrank under former President Biden. That is the last Democratic president’s legacy.

If Democrats think they can bring working-class voters back to their tent because some over-educated angry gender studies majors talk up the miseries of income inequality and the glories of socialism, they are wrong. That may work in the so-called “commie corridors” of Brooklyn, where advanced degrees are as common as cockroaches it will likely fail in purple states.   

In Arizona, which Trump won by more than 5 points in 2024 but lost by a whisker in 2020, Bernie Sanders-endorsed challenger Kai Newkirk was crushed by Democrat incumbent Rep. Greg Stanton (D) in the primary battle over a key House seat. During the campaign, Newkirk followed the Democratic Socialists of America playbook that has worked in azure-blue New York districts, accusing Stanton of being too cozy with Big Business and criticizing him for accepting donations from pro-Israel sources.

The socialist talking points flopped, with the establishment-backed Stanton easily dispatching his rival by taking two-thirds of the vote. That’s just one contest, but it confirms that, so far, the Democratic Socialists of America has been successful in turning already-blue Democratic districts even bluer. Tossing a mainstream Democrat overboard and replacing him with a socialist candidate may increase the sway of radicals in the party, but it hardly reflects a successful outreach to the middle class. 

This discussion has focused on white workers without a college degree, but it is also true that Trump made strides with working-class Hispanics and Blacks in 2024. According to Pew Research, “Trump retained sizable advantages among non-college voters and voters living in rural areas.” Black Democrats, many of whom lean conservative on social and economic issues, are already breaking with the far-left; more may follow. 

Going into the midterms, Trump’s support across most voter groups has dropped, thanks largely to the war with Iran, which is driving up gasoline prices. Democrats will take advantage of that slump, but their forecast gains in November are unlikely to reflect a long-term political reassignment of the American working class. 

Liz Peek is a former partner of major bracket Wall Street firm Wertheim and Company.

data.title: Union helps employer persecute its own members, then finds out
data.description:
Labor unions exist for one simple reason: to protect the rights of their members in the workplace. Workers pay dues expecting that, in return, their union will advocate for and protect them. 

But what happens when a union betrays its own members, actively joining forces with an employer to purge workers over their religious beliefs?

The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit’s recent ruling against Alaska Airlines and the Association of Flight Attendants union tells the story of a union that abandoned its duties and instead functioned as an accomplice to corporate cancel culture.

In early 2021, Alaska Airlines fired flight attendants Marli Brown and Lacey Smith after they expressed religious views over concerns in the workplace. The airline had announced on an internal forum its support for the Equality Act, a proposed bill that would amend federal law in many ways, including opening up women’s spaces to males based upon gender identity and removing protections of the Religious Freedom Restoration Act.

The airline’s post invited comments and questions. In response, both women expressed sincere, faith-based concerns about the legislation. Because of those comments, both were fired. 

Did the flight attendants’ union step in to defend its dues-paying members from wrongful termination? After all, the airline had a legal obligation not to discriminate against its employees based upon their religious beliefs. The union should have challenged these terminations as discriminatory.

Yet rather than serve its purpose by standing up for these two members, union leadership joined the airline in its hostility toward their beliefs.

As the Ninth Circuit’s opinion recounts, the union’s president personally reported the flight attendants’ comments to Alaska Airlines’ leadership and colluded with leadership as to the airline’s proposed response. Union representatives repeatedly disparaged the women in internal union communications, calling them “bigots” and “pukes,” and calling for “repercussions.”

The union representative who later represented Marli even went so far as to ask, “Can we PLEASE get someone to shut down comments, or put Marli and Lacey in a burlap bag and drop them in a well”? 

During oral argument, one of the Ninth Circuit judges observed to the Association of Flight Attendants lawyer that this evidence is “really, really bad for you.” Indeed, the evidence of the union’s extreme hostility toward the flight attendants because of their religious beliefs was overwhelming.

The union’s continual disparagement of Brown and Smith, and its utter failure to raise concerns about religious discrimination, violated federal law. Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibits labor organizations from discriminating, adversely affecting employment, or causing or attempting to cause an employer to discriminate.

As the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission has explained, unions may not “knowingly acquiesce in employment discrimination against their members, join or tolerate employers’ discriminatory practices, or discriminatorily refuse to represent employees’ interests.”

The unanimous Ninth Circuit panel recognized that there was enough evidence that the union failed to oppose employment discrimination to send the case to a jury trial. Labor organizations cannot use progressive corporate initiatives as a smokescreen to strip religious employees of their livelihoods.

As the court observed, a jury could view the union representatives’ actions “as additional evidence that [Association of Flight Attendants] personnel harbored general disagreement with [the] religious beliefs, which may have affected their representation in the disciplinary process.” 

When employees enter a unionized workplace, they do not forfeit their protections under Title VII simply because their religious beliefs are unpopular with union leadership. And they certainly should not be forced to fund a union that turns around and helps management punish them for their faith.

Labor organizations exist to protect workers, not to silence them. As this case shows, when a union stops representing its members and instead acts as a toady for corporate management, the law holds it accountable.

The implications of the Ninth Circuit’s decision stretch far beyond the flight deck of Alaska Airlines. It is a significant reminder for all unions and the workers they represent that civil rights protections apply to unions too. If unions wish to retain the trust of the workers who fund them, they must hold to their core purpose — protecting all employees, not just the ones whose religious beliefs are politically convenient to them. Because when a union fails to represent its members, it betrays its most basic reason for existing in the first place.

Becky Dummermuth is Senior Counsel for First Liberty Institute.
